era_context: The early 10th century CE continues the period of Islamic political fragmentation, Byzantine military resurgence, and East Asian dynastic competition. The Abbasid Caliphate's authority is increasingly nominal; regional dynasties exercise de facto independence. In Western Europe, feudal kingdoms are consolidating. In East Asia, the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period sees constant warfare and territorial realignment.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Americas (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)

events.title: Byzantine military operations in Anatolia
events.description: Byzantine forces conduct military operations in Anatolia to counter Arab raids and consolidate control over frontier regions. These campaigns are part of the broader military revival that will characterise the Macedonian Renaissance. The Byzantines achieve some successes in stabilising the eastern frontier.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine military campaigns in this period are attested in later chronicles. Specific events in 918 are not clearly documented.

events.title: Samanid Emir Nasr II al-Samani consolidates power
events.description: Nasr II al-Samani (r. 914–943) continues to consolidate Samanid control over Central Asia. The Samanids maintain nominal allegiance to the Abbasid Caliph while exercising de facto independence. This period sees the flourishing of Persian literature and Islamic scholarship in Bukhara and Samarkand.
events.certainty_note: Samanid consolidation is well-attested. Specific events in 918 are not clearly documented.

events.title: Khitan (Liao) Dynasty military expansion continues
events.description: Abaoji (r. 907–926) continues military campaigns to consolidate Khitan control over Manchuria and parts of northern China. The Khitan are becoming a major regional power that will challenge Chinese dynasties for centuries.

sources.name: Liao Shi (History of the Liao Dynasty, 13th century)

events.certainty_note: Khitan expansion is attested in later Chinese chronicles. Specific events in 918 are not clearly documented.
cross_references: Part of Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period

events.title: Later Liang Dynasty continues to decline in northern China
events.description: The Later Liang Dynasty continues to weaken as rival military commanders and regional warlords challenge its authority. The Later Liang will be displaced by the Later Tang Dynasty in 923 CE. This period is marked by constant warfare and political instability.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government, 11th century)

events.certainty_note: The decline of Later Liang is well-attested. Specific events in 918 are not clearly documented.
cross_references: Part of Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–960)

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Muqtafi maintains nominal authority
events.description: Al-Muqtafi (r. 908–932) continues to reign nominally while regional dynasties exercise de facto power. The Abbasid Caliphate is increasingly a religious and symbolic authority rather than a political power.
events.certainty_note: Al-Muqtafi's reign is well-documented in Islamic chronicles. The year 918 falls within his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the significance of 918 as a turning point in any of these regions is not clearly established in surviving sources. The year appears to be a relatively quiet year in the historical record, with no major events specifically documented.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 918 CE is moderate in quality but notably sparse for this specific year. Byzantine and Islamic sources are relatively abundant but mostly compiled centuries after the events. Chinese sources are similarly retrospective. The year 918 does not appear to be marked by any major events in surviving sources; it represents a period of gradual change rather than dramatic turning points. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military narratives; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Byzantine military operations in 918 contribute to later victories
Decline of Later Liang in 918 leads to Later Tang's establishment in 923
